steps.title: Identify the two clauses
steps.content:
Clause 1: "In many languages, including English, these consonants are [blank]" — a complete independent clause once the blank is filled. Clause 2: "in some dialects of Mandarin, however, four such consonants are relatively common" — also a complete independent clause. Two independent clauses in one sentence need a semicolon (or comma + coordinating conjunction) between them.
  • Choice A — rare and: The conjunction "and" conflicts with "however" later in the sentence, creating an illogical double connective. ✗
  • Choice B — rare,: A comma alone joining two independent clauses is a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — rare: No punctuation between two independent clauses creates a run-on sentence. ✗
  • Choice D — rare ;: A semicolon correctly separates the two independent clauses without conflicting with "however." ✓

steps.title: Confirm the semicolon rule
steps.content: A semicolon joins two closely related independent clauses — exactly what is needed here. D is correct.
